paragraphs.body: I want to do it on the dopamine you feel running track and why you cant get that anywhere else. I want to change it to that topic now because I feel like it is so much more relevant in my life now, and the audience to that essay would be my coaches/family because I recently had my event majorly changed from running sprints to throwing. And it has been Absolut horrible like worst 2 months of my life. I am not good at it. I am not traveling, and I am so board. I have never felt this way about track before and when I try to explain it nobody gets it and it is extremely frustrating so if I can relate this back to my situation anyway to get someone to understand where I am coming from I would be really happy and I feel like I could write about this topic for hours because I have e so much to say about it and its so new

annotations.text: And it has been Absolut horrible like worst 2 months of my life. I am not good at it. I am not traveling, and I am so board

paragraphs.body: Growing up I did lots of sports, the main ones that I stuck with where basketball, cheer, and track. From ages 6 to now if you asked any what my favorite sport is everyone would have the same answer, “track.” There were lots of things that I enjoyed about track that I did not get from anywhere else. The first one would be that it was a sport that I did not have to think about at all. With basketball you have to think about what plays your doing and basically how to always be one step ahead. And although I don’t think cheer is really a sport, there were a lot of things I had to think about in cheer too, I had to know all the cheers and dances as well as really think about my technique when it came to stunting and tumbling. But with track I did not have to think about anything. Track was just running as fast as you can and than it was over. Another thing I enjoyed about track is that what you put into it is how much you got back. This means that no one can cheat in track, how hard you work is going to show up on the track during race day. And Finally this one might be a little shallow but, another thing that I liked about track is how effortlessly the sport came to me. It was something that I was very good at and it made me feel really good about myself and all the hard work I put in to get to this point. Nothing else is life has ever come that easily to me.

annotations.text: But with track I did not have to think about anything. Track was just running as fast as you can and than it was over.
annotations.comment: There's something very lovely about this description! I hear your voice coming through, here.

annotations.text: his means that no one can cheat in track, how hard you work is going to show up on the track during race day. paragraphs.body: There’s also nothing like the feeling when you are about to run a race. It all starts for me at least a few days before. The routine I took nights before to prepare me for track was to find the heat sheets and look at every girl who was in my heat and every time they had ever run. A lot of coaches say not to do this because it usually stresses competitors out, but to me it gave me a since of peace knowing exactly who was in my heat. Not that it mattered though because in track anything can happen. Someone can be having a really good day and run a time they never thought was possible, or somebody could be having a really bad day and not put out the times that everyone is expecting from them. But anyways continuing to my race day routine. I’m jumping ahead to the night before race day, this part for me is really important and it starts when I’m laying in bed waiting to fall asleep but I can’t because I’m more exited for the meet than Christmas Day. And you have to lay down and invision the race, and what you need to do to execute and win. Next part of my routine for race day is the morning before, this part changed in between middle school to high school. I always had to have the same breakfast every morning on race day. It was a bowl of oatmeal with a alani nu energy drink or a zaur energy drink. After I ate breakfast and sipped my energy drink it was time for one of my favorite parts, the getting ready process. The motto I had for running was look good, feel good, run good. This would be the time when I experimented with new hairstyles and put on my makeup while blasting my favorite music at the time. At this point I used so much time and it was time to go. No matter how far away the meet was I needed my headphones. I always listened to music on the bus. I am a very talkative person but before I run the 100 meter dash I would barley say a word. This is when I was the most focused. Now we are at about an hour before a race. I am warming up and doing, my runouts. (These are when you model the beginning of the race and run as fast as you can for about 30 meters. Now I’m about 10 minutes before the race starts and we’re lined u

paragraphs.body: And I, watching the people before me go, while jumping up and down and taking a runout in between every meet. Finally it’s my turn to run. When I set up my blocks I don't us my feet to measure out my blocks like most people do.Instead I memorized my numbers and would just put them in that setting. Than I would take a small runout in my blocks before the oﬃcial blows this whistle and says “on your marks” this is when I finally get into my blocks and ,my heart's beating really fast but I a, so exited and no feeling compares to the one that’s happening at that moment “set” and than the gun goes oﬀ and I don't hear anything. Up to the collegiate level 9/10 I did not see anyone, I was also so focused that I never. Heard anyone either. I was just running as fast as I could. Finally I crossed that race(it was Usually a win) I look at the clock and then walked oﬀ the track and then my talkative personality comes back, and that’s the perfect description for my races and why no other sport can compare to that. The feeling that I get after a meet day is almost indescribable. ““Exercise has a dramatic antidepressive effect, ” says Linden. “It blunts the brain’s response to physical and emotional stress” I truly believe that running does release an antidepressant because if the meet was good. I’ll even say decently that the whole trajectory of my week has changed. I genuinely will be so happy until it’s time to prepare for the next meet. I don’t feel this at all with throwing, it is quite the opposite for throwing I feel absolute dread, and the opposite of dopamine. Honestly I think throwing releases anti dopamine.

annotations.comment: I'm also interested in "Look good. Feel good. Run good." Let's talk about that, too :)
annotations.context_left: absolute dread, and the opposite of dopamine. Honestly I think throwing releases anti dopamine.

annotations.text: Finally I crossed that race(it was Usually a win)
